data.title: Tiziana: Scope Of Foralumab Goes Beyond That Of Multiple Sclerosis Targeting
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Tiziana Life Sciences (NASDAQ:TLSA) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Tiziana: Foralumab Drug Development Presses On With Additional EAP Enrollment". With respect to this article, I noted that the company was working on advancing the use of its fully human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody foralumab for the treatment of patients with non-active secondary progressive multiple sclerosis [naSPMS] This is being done so in an open-label trial Expanded Access [EA] program. It was shown that patients given this drug over a 6-month period either saw an improvement or stabilization of their disease.

Not only that, but that the company is already in the process of developing this anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody drug in a phase 2a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study targeting these patients with naSPMS. This time around, I believe it is important to go over another promising program from the company's pipeline, which is that this fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody can also be developed to target patients with mild-cognitive impairment due to early-stage Alzheimer's Disease [AD].

More about this below, but the key is to target the microglial activation that causes inflammation found in these patients with this progressive neurodegenerative disorder. Why do I believe it is important to bring this up? Well, it is because there is a catalyst approaching in the near-term, which would be the initiation of a phase 2 study using foralumab to treat these patients with early-stage AD at some point in 2025. As far as the phase 2a randomized double-blind study goes, using this drug to target patients with naSPMS, data from this trial is expected to be released in 2026. However, the CEO is expected to present recent clinical findings for this MS treatment program at the Bio International Convention on June 18th 2025 at 11:45 AM ET.

A Second Indication To Explore With Foralumab On The Horizon

As I noted above, the company is in the process of running both its open-label Expanded Access [EA] program and its phase 2a randomized, double-blind study using foralumab to treat patients with non-active secondary progressive multiple sclerosis [naSPMS]. The beauty of this anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody is that it could be used to target a multitude of other neurodegenerative and neuroinflammatory disorders beyond that of only Multiple Sclerosis [MS]. With that being said, the company is diving the use of developing intranasal foralumab to target patients with mild cognitive impairment with early-stage Alzheimer's Disease. Before going over the expected trial in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from this specific program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for Tiziana Life Sciences could end up being. Alzheimer's Disease [AD] is a type of neurodegenerative disorder whereby there is a progressive loss of nerve cells [neurons] in the brain due to neurofibrillary tangles in the brain. Plus, there is a buildup of amyloid plaques, which cause brain cells to die off. In essence, the brain cells being lost causes the brain to shrink and leads to inflammation as well. With that being said, this disorder can cause memory loss, behavior issues and thinking. The global Alzheimer's Disease market is expected to reach $23.8 billion by 2031. This is a very large market opportunity for the company to go after.

In order to see whether or not fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody intranasal foralumab can be used effectively to target mild-cognitive impairment due to early-stage AD, the company is expected to initiate a phase 2 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to assess the safety and efficacy of this drug for the treatment of these patients. What is known about the study itself is that it is listed as "not yet recruiting" because the study has not yet begun. The company believes that it is expected to begin this phase 2 study at some point during this year in 2025. Specifically, this study is expected to randomize patients 3:1 to receive one of the following doses:

  • 50 micrograms of nasal foralumab
  • 100 micrograms of nasal foralumab
  • Placebo

It is important to note that one of these forms of treatments is to be given in three-week "treatment cycles". In essence, the goal is to give three doses of this drug per week for 2 weeks and then have 1 week as a rest period. Two important primary outcome measures being looked at are adverse events [AEs] and microglial activation observed with PET scan imaging from baseline to 20-weeks.

Why the need for the latter efficacy endpoint, I noted? Before going into this, it is first important to note what I stated above, which is that AD is caused due to several factors, which are:

  • Amyloid plaques
  • Neurofibrillary tangles
  • Microglial activation

The last one noted, microglial activation, is what causes inflammation or disease burden in patients with Alzheimer's Disease [AD]. Thus, the point of ameliorating disease burden is to reduce the amount of microglial activation that takes place. Then, how does foralumab as a fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody help in this regard? It does so by modulating regulatory T-cells to dampen microglial inflammation. The potential is to reduce inflammation to the point that a patient doesn't have a lot of microglial activation in place to affect their thinking, memory and behavior. This factor of reduced microglial activation ameliorating disease burden of AD was shown in a murine model. Not only was this shown, but it was done so independent of amyloid beta deposition.

Thus, not only can the company initiate a phase 2 study to use foralumab to treat these patients with early-stage AD, but it can do so in combination with an anti-amyloid therapy as well. The bottom line is that it can go beyond that of only using this intranasal anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody alone to target neuroinflammation associated with AD. Speaking of the intranasal route of administration, the Tregs are modulated locally; thus there is no systemic toxicity observed in using foralumab to inhibit microglial activation because of the route of administration being done. What remains to be seen now with the soon-to-be initiated phase 2 randomized, double-blind study is that the theory of using Tregs to modulate microglial activation does result in reduced inflammation. Thus, in tandem, brings about improved cognition for these patients with early-stage AD. With all this being said, the company believes that it can initiate the phase 2 randomized study, using foralumab to treat these AD patients, at some point during this year in 2025.

Financials

According to the 20-F/A SEC Filing, Tiziana Life Sciences had cash and cash equivalents of $3.72 million as of December 31st of 2024. It doesn't have a lot of cash on hand, it is highly likely that it will need to raise funds at least at some point this year. The reason why I state this is because in its 20-F/A SEC Filing, it notes that it believes it has enough to fund its operating expenses and capital expenditures for the immediate future. Not only that, but it lists a risk that it may be forced to scale back its operations if necessary to conserve cash and carry its pipeline forward. This would likely end up involving to halt further development of one of its ongoing studies to conserve cash. It is burning through a lot of cash each year; thus it is going to need to continue raising capital as often as it can through progression of its studies. For the year ending December 31, 2024 it burned through 15.79 million in cash. This is broken down into $5.22 million in R&D expenses and then $10.56 million in operating expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Tiziana Life Sciences. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the development of the ongoing phase 2 study, using foralumab for the treatment of patients with naSPMS. The company is expected to report top-line data from this mid-stage trial at some point in 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the results to be released from this study are going to be positive for starters. Nor, that the company will be able to receive authorization from regulators to move this program towards the next stage of clinical testing.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the soon-to-be initiated phase 2 study using foralumab for the treatment of mild cognitive impairment in patients with early-stage Alzheimer's Disease [AD]. Upon initiation, along with eventual completion of this trial, there is no assurance that the mechanism of action [MOA] of locally inducing Tregs [T-regulatory cells] will result in the control of microglial activation in the brain. The hope is that an anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody given via an intranasal route of administration results in disease reduction burden for these patients with AD.

The third and final risk to consider would be the company's cash position. As I laid out above, it doesn't have enough cash on hand, given the fact that it only ended December 31st of 2024 with only $3.72 million in cash. With that being said, it is highly likely that it is going to need to raise cash in the next several months. This is going to be through the use of equity offerings, as noted in the 20-F/A SEC Filing. Plus, there is also the risk noted that it could possibly scale backs its operations. This would be to reduce ongoing studies or halt development of specific clinical drugs from its pipeline.

Conclusion

Tiziana Life Sciences is continuing to progress the use of its fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody foralumab for the treatment of patients with naSPMS. This program entails the advancement of two ongoing studies, which are the open-label Expanded Access [EA] program trial and the phase 2 randomized, double-blind placebo controlled one. It is not much longer to see the completion of the mid-stage randomized study because the projection is that the company could release data from it at some point in 2026.

One near-term catalyst to focus on though would be the initiation of a phase 2 study using foralumab to treat patients with mild cognitive impairment due to early-stage AD. This is expected to begin shortly, and the hope is that the company establishes proof-of-concept in using an intranasal anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody like foralumab effectively uses Tregs to modulate microglial cells.

Plus, you also have the upcoming presentation of prior data released by the CEO of this drug targeting patients with naSPMS. Such results will be shown at the upcoming Bio International Convention to be held on June 18, 2025 at 11:45 AM ET. With mid-stage trial data expected from foralumab program targeting patients with naSPMS in 2026, plus the initiation of a phase 2 study targeting patients with early-stage AD on the way, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: RCI Hospitality: Several Concerning Trends To Note (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
RCI Hospitality Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:RICK), the nightclub operator and Bombshells restaurant chain owner, has reported concerning earnings. Nightclubs have shown weaker sales due to several transitory factors, but decreasing alcohol consumption also acts as a more persistent threat to RCI Hospitality’s core business. At the same time, Bombshells’ slight turnaround signals have faded.

I remained at a Buy rating in my previous January article on the stock, titled “RCI Hospitality: Back To Basics”. The stock has since returned -22%, underperforming the S&P 500’s return of 5%.

RCI Hospitality’s Performance Is Weakening

RCI Hospitality’s fiscal Q2 report from early 2025 showed quite a subdued performance. Both the nightclub segment and Bombshells showed concerning earnings trends, albeit some of the reasons for the performance are transitory.

The nightclub segment had a suddenly weaker performance. Total segment revenues were reported at $57.5 million, down by -3.1% year-on-year due to a noteworthy -3.5% decline in same-store sales. As a result of weaker revenues, segment earnings also declined. Nightclubs’ adjusted operating income came in at $17.1 million for the quarter, down by -13.5% year-on-year through sales deleverage and other negative factors. Overall, earnings still stayed at a healthy level with a 29.8% operating margin, but the decline in earnings is notable regardless for a historically quite stable segment.

Some of the segment decline is due to transitory reasons, not being too concerning over the long term. Other entertainment venue operators also reported weak traffic for the period, as I have written on companies such as Topgolf Callaway (MODG) and Lucky Strike (LUCK) (articles: MODG, LUCK). Consumer confidence has been lower throughout the period, causing more cautious consumer activity. On top, traffic had a hiccup due to weak weather during the quarter, and a fire in RCI Hospitality’s Fort Worth location caused further disruption to the revenue performance. Restaurant chains’ traffic also reflects a weak nationwide trend in especially February. The mentioned factors should ultimately turn out temporary as consumer confidence should eventually rebound, and as weather conditions normalize and operations are restored in Fort Worth.

The nightclub segment’s performance does also warrant some caution over the longer term. Notably, alcohol sales declined by -5.3% for Q2, being a critical part of the nightclub business. The decline represents a secular trend that could turn out to be concerning for RCI Hospitality – young people are drinking less alcohol, as has for example recently been noted by Brown-Forman (BF.B). Spirits companies’ and many brewers’ sales trend already reflects the fact, and RCI Hospitality’s performance is now following. Lower alcohol consumption could continue to weigh on RCI Hospitality's alcohol sales, slowly pressuring traffic over the long term.

The Bombshells chain had a concerning quarter – same-store sales declined by -13.4%, halting some previous turnaround progress. The segment previously showed a much lower -7.5% decline in the metric in Q1, but Bombshells decline has now reaccelerated. Given that RCI Hospitality has recently divested five underperforming locations, but Q2 still showed such a sharp decline in comparable sales, the chain’s turnaround efforts seem very unsuccessful. Bombshells’ adjusted operating income declined to a negative -$0.1 million level from $0.8 million a year ago, as cost savings aren’t able to offset such a deep sales decline.

Notably, some of the Bombshells decline is clearly also temporary; as told, bad weather caused weak industry-wide restaurant traffic in early 2025. Yet, such a sharp decline in same-store sales hasn’t been reported by any peer that I know of – McDonald’s (MCD) -3.6% same-store sales decline in the period has been noted as very weak, still less than a third of Bombshells’.

RCI Hospitality is still developing Bombshells locations, with a Denver opening during Q2 and additional ones in Lubbock and Rowlett to open soon. Although no new developments seem likely after current construction, the new locations only add to a concerning segment outlook as traffic continues to plummet in Bombshells locations. RCI Hospitality targets Bombshells to recover to a 15% operating margin and positive same-store sales growth, but based on the current trend, I am skeptical of Bombshells’ future.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for RCI Hospitality’s stock to estimate a fair value. I now estimate slightly slower revenue growth than previously due to weaker same-store sales growth expectations, where RCI Hospitality now reaches $384.2 million in FY2029 revenues compared to the company’s $400 million target. The revenue estimates now suggest a 4.9% 2024-2034 CAGR through acquisitions and slow same-store sales growth, afterwards ending at stable 2% perpetual growth.

Due to RCI Hospitality’s weaker earnings performance, I now also estimate margins more cautiously. I estimate the EBIT margin to stay stagnant at a 18% level going forward, not suggesting a major earnings rebound for Bombshells.

I again factor expected nightclub acquisitions into cash flow estimates, weakening especially the next couple of years’ cash flow figures.

The estimates put RCI Hospitality’s fair value at $50.34, 20% above the stock price at the time of writing. The lower stock price still creates some upside in the valuation. Due to RCI Hospitality’s unclear earnings prospects, especially for Bombshells, though, I believe that the upside now merely represents a fair margin of safety – I don’t consider the lower price an attractive entry point. The stock is now cheap, mostly for good reason.

The fair value estimate is down from $70.82 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.42%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.71% interest rate, annualized from Q2 interest expenses and debt. I again assume quite a significant 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.38% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimates for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.13. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity stands at 11.17% and the WACC at 9.42%.

Takeaway

RCI Hospitality’s earnings are weakening. The nightclub segment’s performance has reflected several transitory weaknesses, but also a secular decline in alcohol consumption that looks to pressure the segment going forward as well. Bombshells’ turnaround isn’t progressing despite the divestment of underperforming locations. Due to weaker earnings expectations, I don’t believe the stock to be a clearly attractive investment anymore. As such, I now downgrade my rating on RCI Hospitality to Hold.

data.title: Unemployment Claims Unchanged From Previous Week, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Initial jobless claims measure the number of people who file for unemployment for the first time in a given week. In the week ending June 7th, initial jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 248,000, remaining at their highest level in eight months. This is unchanged from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was higher than the 242,000 forecast.

Here is the complete data series dating back to 1967. There is a good bit of volatility in this indicator, which is why the four-week moving average is a more useful number than the weekly data because it smooths short-term fluctuations to highlight the overall trend. The four-week moving average currently stands at 240,250 - up 5,000 from the previous week and the highest level in nine months.

Outside of the COVID spike, initial unemployment claims have never been greater than 700,000 for a given week, making the chart above less useful for identifying trends. To address this, we've adjusted the y-axis on the chart below to provide a closer view of the data, minimizing the impact of the COVID surge.

Notice the relationship between recessions and the rise in weekly unemployment claims. To no surprise, the 4-week moving average begins to rise at or before the start of a recession and peaks around its conclusion.

In another attempt to eliminate the COVID spike, the chart below shows initial unemployment claims starting in October 2021.

Initial Unemployment Claims: Not Seasonally Adjusted

The headline unemployment insurance data - and the charts above - are seasonally adjusted. But what does the non-seasonally adjusted data look like? The chart below highlights its extreme volatility, as shown by the green dots. The four-week moving average helps reveal the recurring seasonal patterns, such as the regular spikes in January.

Because of the extreme volatility of the non-adjusted weekly data, we can add a 52-week moving average to give a better sense of the secular trends. The chart below also has a linear regression through the data (red line), which we are currently below.

Continuing Unemployment Claims

Continuing unemployment claims are the number of people who have already filed for unemployment and have continued to claim benefits for a subsequent week. Unlike initial unemployment claims, continuing claims are not considered a leading economic indicator and are a less important measure of economic health.

Additionally, data for continuing claims has a one-week lag compared to initial claims. In the week ending May 31st, continuing jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 1,956,000, the highest level since November 2021. This represents an increase of 54,000 from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was higher than the 1,910,000 forecast.

Here is the data series dating back to 1967. Again, we've included the 4-week moving average to highlight the overall trend. The four-week moving average currently stands at 1,914,500 - an increase of 19,750 from the previous week and the highest level since November 2021.

Here is a zoomed in view so that the COVID spike is not as prominent.

And lastly, here is a chart starting in November 2021 to see the more recent trends in continuing claims.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boeing Has Clearer Skies Ahead But The Turnaround Is Not Over
data.description:
Kenio Fontes co-authored this article

Like a boxer that has survived the fight after being knocked down multiple times, Boeing (NYSE:BA) is still hanging in there. And unless the world order changes even more dramatically than it already has (and that's a lot!), BA should continue to be the most prominent aerospace company west of the Atlantic Ocean.

There are many reasons to feel like the worst is over for BA. For the business, that is. The stock is still part of a broader market, one that operates very differently now than it used to. But we conclude that BA has potential to be one of those stocks we look back at 10 years from now and say that regardless of what the market did, it was competitive.

With that backdrop, here's the technical view, followed by the fundamental and business aspects of the BA thesis.

First, unless this company is going away, we can't help but notice that its 10-year annualized return is about as low as it has been. 2.6% a year for 10 years incorporates the impact of the pandemic, several highly visible safety issues, union problems and tariffs, and more. If the long-term past is a long-term prologue, this stock, which has not had a 10-year negative return, is showing signs of long-term seller exhaustion. And with 8-16% annualized compounded return as a past reference range, the upside outweighs the down here.

The technical picture is also encouraging, though there could be some bumps in the road. We first covered BA back on December 11, 2024. And while we can be stingy with buy ratings, this one was already short-term washed out. Now, while it is not nearly as attractive near-term as it was back then, its longer-term positioning has bypassed some crisis moments.

This very long-term (monthly) chart indicates to me that if BA can burst through that upcoming downtrend around $210-$215, it could be ready to be a leader within the Dow Industrials, of which it is 1 of 30 member stocks.

However, the reaction to the Air India crash may have delayed that departure through that higher level for a while. The shorter-term daily chart (below) indicates to me that there's the risk of the market re-assessing BA at above $200 a share. BA has bumped its noggin on the overhead bin, so to speak, and is now looking toppy near-term.

The Path Is Complex But Less Cloudy Now

On the fundamental side, the first thing we have to keep in mind is that Boeing is a complex operation, and operations impact fundamentals, which impact price. The challenges mentioned, such as safety issues, impacted the financials.

For instance, Q1 was an improvement in a number of indicators, but the company still posted negative free cash flow of $2.3 billion and an operating margin of 2.4%. The chart below not only proves that the financials are quite compressed, but also shows us something very important for the thesis: operating leverage.

Boeing is very dependent on higher volume in order to dilute its high costs. E.g., with 2019 revenue, the net income margin could reach 10%, but when production and demand fall due to a number of factors, this acts as a counter-effect, with operational deleveraging greatly impacting results.

This already gives us a main trigger: the normalization/increase in production. In Q1, it was mentioned that 737 production is gradually increasing, and the expectation is to reach 38 per month by 2025, and closer to the end of the year to reach 42 per month, as mentioned in the earnings call. Higher production means a higher margin and, consequently, an enhancement in the shareholder value being generated.

The backlog is another positive point in the thesis, with $460 billion for commercial airplanes and $62 billion for defense, space & security, which was increased after winning the F-47 contract.

This adds greater predictability, but only as a proxy for gross revenue for the coming years, since converting this into margin still depends on other factors such as optimizations, production ramp-up, and the like. A reasonable proxy is to imagine that by converting $80 billion per year in revenue, there are a little fewer than six years to explore this backlog for commercial airplanes, and roughly speaking, if the consolidated net margin were 6%, we could expect a net income of $4.8 billion per year.

The balance sheet, in my view, is a double-edged sword. As the chart below shows, the company has close to $30 billion in net debt. This is a very considerable amount, especially for a company that has had high cash burn in the last 12 months.

But looking on the bright side, in addition to making the thesis more complex and riskier, the debt also becomes a trigger. In the earnings call, it was mentioned that in addition to access to $10 billion in revolving credit facilities, Boeing also has an agreement to sell part of its digital aviation solutions business for a similar amount. All of these are triggers for reducing net debt and strengthening the balance sheet.

In short, the company will still have to prove that it can resume production, adjust its balance sheet, and return to the level of income it once delivered, but the signs are already positive enough to remove some of the (relevant) uncertainties from the thesis.

Valuation: Good For A Turnaround, But Not Great

Trying to estimate a fair price for companies that are in a turnaround is always tricky, and in addition to this disclaimer, it is necessary to mention that the uncertainty here is even higher due to cyclicality.

Right off the bat, when looking at the market cap, it is clear that the valuation here is not so great. In mid-2019, which was a period when the company was able to deliver excellent fundamentals, such as a free cash flow of $13 billion, the market cap was $240 billion, i.e., the price-to-FCF multiple was 18x.

Now, after the rebound, the market cap has reached $160 billion. Note that the fundamentals are nowhere near what they once were, but the market cap has nevertheless managed to return to a more appropriate level. This occurred due to the factors mentioned in the previous section; the company now has a slightly clearer path.

Therefore, I don't think we can call the valuation here great or believe that there is tremendous asymmetry. But still, with the triggers mentioned, there is potential for appreciation.

The EV-to-EBITDA, for example, is 37x. With a higher level of production, transforming the backlog into more and more revenue, the EBITDA margin should increase substantially. Balance sheet initiatives, on the other hand, can reduce net debt, i.e., they can decrease enterprise value. With higher EBITDA and lower EV, the multiple should compress, or the market cap may rise to compensate for these improvements, which is expected.

The Bottom Line

There are potential gains to be captured in BA stock. As the technical picture shows, with a normalization of the scenario, the stock should return to producing more decent gains. The valuation also shows that if the company normalizes production and continues to execute its initiatives, free cash flow may once again show considerable volumes, and this should translate into a higher market cap and/or a higher shareholder yield.

The problem is that although the triggers and the path are now clearer, it is still complex and presents significant challenges, especially in the short and medium term, while the long term seems clearer due to the strength of the brand, the company's positioning, and trends such as demand for aviation. Therefore, risk management is even more advisable here.

data.title: Petrobras: A Value Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Petroleo Brasileiro (NYSE: PBR) is a Brazilian oil and gas company. This article will show why PBR could be an exciting buy at the moment, despite the political risk. Furthermore, I will demonstrate why the upcoming election could be an opportunity for higher valuations for Petrobras.

Fundamentals

Petrobras is a very profitable company, with its gross profit margin of just under 50% and its EBITDA margin of almost 40% significantly higher than the industry median. Furthermore, the company has a higher than average ROE of 11.98%.

The company's only problem is its growth, which is, as most people would expect from an energy company, very low at only 1.14% revenue growth and even negative EBITDA growth of -16.1%. All of this is worse than the industry average.

Nevertheless, the company's biggest strength is its valuation. Currently, it only has an EV/EBITDA ratio of 3.76 and a forward one of 3.1, which is a discount of 42.08% and 45.24% to the sector, respectively. Furthermore, the P/B and P/S ratios of 0.99 and 0.79 are far below the sector average, too. Lastly, the P/E ratio of 8.13 implies an earnings yield of 12.3%, which would be a very attractive return. Figure 1 shows the historic P/E ratio of PBR. As can be seen, the current valuation is in the lower historical segment.

Peer Group Comparison

When comparing PBR to competitors (Figure 2) like Exxon Mobil (NYSE: XOM), BP (NYSE: BP), and Shell (NYSE: SHEL), we can instantly see that the company is much cheaper than its peers at a premium of sometimes even 50%. The P/S ratio is the only metric where PBR is more expensive than Shell and BP, while PBR shows the lowest value in every other valuation category. Additionally, PBR's growth of 1.14% is still the second best in the group, with only Exxon Mobil being slightly higher, but at a very big premium of 95% according to the EV/EBITDA ratio. Nevertheless, the negative EBITDA growth remains a weakness. Only BP shows more contraction in this field at -33.04%, while Exxon even has positive growth of 2.82%. Considering profitability, Petrobras has the best gross profit margin. Additionally, the company's EBITDA margin of 39.54% is more than double that of its competitors, which have one between 13.73% (BP) and 19.02% (XOM). All of this suggests that Petrobras is a great quality company and one of the best in its peer group at a very cheap price. So why is the stock so cheap? The reason for this is likely to be due to political risk in Brazil.

Evaluating Brazil's Political Risk

When comparing an American ETF like SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA: SPY) to a Brazilian one like the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (NYSEARCA: EWZ), one can instantly see the discount Brazilian stocks trade at. While SPY has a P/E ratio of 22.98 and a P/B ratio of 4.77, EWZ only has values of 9.46 and 1.53, respectively, showing a discount of more than 50%, which is very likely the reason for Petrobras' cheap valuation. Because of this, it makes sense to assess the potential risk for the specific case of Petrobras.

The current ruling party of Brazil is the Workers Party (PT), which is in favor of state intervention and has job creation as one of its main goals. While they do not have a stable majority in Congress, they still pose a big risk as they might use price controls that could damage Petrobras. For the upcoming election in 2026, the center-right Liberal Party (PL) is leading in polls with 37.6%, while the Workers Party only has the support of 33.6%. In contrast to the Workers Party, the PL is more market-friendly and wants less intervention, which could boost Petrobras and Brazilian stocks in general to higher valuations.

While the current government is a risk, one still has to remember that Petrobras has gone through many different legislations that partly nationalized the company, yet it still does well. When combining this with the unstable majority in Congress, it is unlikely that the company will face any fatal political shifts, making the political risk manageable, especially with the chance of a more business-friendly new government.

From an economic perspective, Brazil also looks interesting at the moment, with unemployment at 6.6%, one of the lowest levels in the last 5 years, as can be seen in Figure 3. Furthermore, GDP is growing at 2.9%, which is in the country's usual range, while inflation is elevated at 5.53% but still manageable. At the same time, interest rates at 14.75% are currently rising, which could mean some short-term pain for PBR.

Technicals

From a technical perspective, PBR is clearly in a downtrend as can be seen in Figure 4. The stock is currently below its 200-day and 50-day simple moving averages. Furthermore, it does have negative 6-month and 1-year momentum of -21.83% and -23.11%, respectively. All of this suggests buying PBR in tranches to participate in further potential price decreases.

Conclusion

In total, Petrobras looks like a very interesting buy at the moment. While there could be some more short-term pain, the upcoming election could be a great chance for investors. Furthermore, Petrobras has very strong fundamentals at a very attractive valuation and an earnings yield of over 12%. All of this makes the company currently a buy for me, which could become a strong buy at even lower prices.

data.title: Corvus Pharmaceuticals: Multiple Opportunities, But Plenty Of Risk
data.description:
Investment Overview - What's Changed At Corvus Since My Last Coverage, In 2021?

In a note for Seeking Alpha, I was bullish back in January 2021 on Corvus (NASDAQ:CRVS), although I downgraded stock to a hold later that year, in September, after a massive surge in the company's stock price, from $2.3, to $6.9, as data shared by Pharma AstraZeneca (AZN), related to its post-chemo stage III non-small cell lung cancer ("NSCLC') drug Imfinzi, showed improved efficacy when used in combo with an anti-CD73 MaB.

Corvus was developing just such an asset, an anti-CD73 candidate named Mupadolimab, but Corvus's gains were short-lived, and shares sank <$1 in March 2023.

In its 2024 10K Corvus states that:

While we believe mupadolimab has the potential to be an important new therapeutic agent with a novel mechanism of action for the treatment of a broad range of cancers and infectious diseases, we are waiting to initiate a potential Phase 2 randomized clinical trial in order to prioritize the development of our other two lead product candidates. Angel Pharmaceuticals is continuing the development of mupadolimab in China.

Angel Pharma is Corvus' China-based partner. AstraZeneca's anti-CD73 asset oleclumab is still being trialled alongside Imfinzi, with results expected in 2026, the company's 2024 10K reveals. If that data set impresses, perhaps Corvus will be able to secure a partner to advance studies of mupadolimab.

Meanwhile, in September last year, Corvus stock spiked again, this time to $9.5 per share, as the company, according to Seeking Alpha:

announced the beginning of a Phase 3 registrational trial for its lead candidate, soquelitinib, in peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL). The global trial is expected to enroll about 150 patients with relapsed/refractory PTCL at approximately 40 sites in countries including the U.S. and Canada.

The trial designed to evaluate soquelitinib as a late-line option will assess progression-free survival as its primary goal, while overall survival, objective response rate, and duration of response will be among some of its secondary goals.

Here is how Corvus discusses its lead asset in its Q1 2025 quarterly report / 10Q submission:

We have designed and developed soquelitinib to covalently target the cysteine amino acid residue at position 442 in the ITK protein. We believe this irreversible targeting of ITK has the potential to provide a potent, selective and prolonged duration of activity without the need for high systemic exposures and thereby improve the therapeutic window. This approach was previously used by our cofounders to generate ibrutinib.

Ibrutinib was developed by Pharmacyclics under the leadership of Robert Duggan, now CEO of Summit Therapeutics (SMMT), and then sold to AbbVie (ABBV) in a deal worth >$20bn in 2015. Corvus' CEO Richard Miller was a co-founder of Pharmacyclics, and Leiv Lea its Chief Financial Officer for a spell.

Examining Soquelitinib In Oncology, Autoimmune

According to Corvus' 10Q:

In March 2025, updated clinical results of the Phase 1/1b trial were presented at the T Cell Lymphoma Forum. For the 23 evaluable patients:

  • Objective responses (CRs plus PRs) were seen in nine patients (39%), including six CRs (26%) and three PRs.
  • The median duration of response for the nine patients with objective response by Lugano criteria was 17.2m.
  • Three patients continue on therapy at 25+ months, 18+ months and 14+ months
  • Kaplan Meier estimated median progression free survival (“PFS”) was 6.2 months.
  • At 18-month follow-up, the PFS rate was 30%, which compares favorably to 18-month PFS of <20% with belinostat or pralatrexate.

The data did not seem to blow analysts away, as Corvus' stock, which had already dropped back to ~$4.5 per share, retreated to <$3 per share.

No other companies appear to be focused on ITK inhibition in oncology indications, and according to Corvus goes pivotal in peripheral T-cell lymphoma:

Perhaps the more relevant bar to clear is the 44% ORR seen with both Dizal’s JAK1 inhibitor golidocitinib and Daiichi Sankyo’s EZH1/2 inhibitor valemetostat in late-line PTCL, in global mid-stage studies presented separately at last year’s ASH meeting. Haihe Biopharma’s HH2853 produced an even more impressive 65% ORR, albeit in a smaller phase 1 Chinese trial.

Golidocitinib and valemetostat were approved for relapsed/refractory PTCL in China and Japan respectively in June; their paths forward in the US are less clear.

With soquelitinib having achieved 39% ORR, the success of a Phase 3 study, let alone a successful approval is by no means guaranteed, while the market opportunity is likely <$1bn per annum.

In the immunology field, Corvus recently shared Phase 1 data from a study of soquelitinib in atopic dermatitis, an autoimmune indication, and a multi-billion dollar market.

Management revealed:

earlier and deeper responses in cohort 3 (200 mg twice per day, total daily dose 400 mg) compared to cohorts 1 and 2 (100 mg twice per day and 200 mg once per day, total daily dose 200 mg).

Overall, all three cohorts showed significant responses in the soquelitinib treatment groups compared to placebo for clinically significant endpoints of EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index) 75 and IGA (Investigator Global Assessment) 0 or 1. Soquelitinib was well tolerated, with no dose limiting toxicities (DLTs).

In cohort 3, the percent reduction in mean EASI score at 28 days was 71.1% for patients receiving soquelitinib and 42.1% for patients receiving placebo.

As we can see above (source: Corvus presentation), Corvus believes soquelitinib's ability to effect multiple inflammatory pathways may give it a competitive edge over the likes of Dupixent, or Rinvoq. This is a bold claim indeed, however - last year, Rinvoq earned nearly $6bn of revenues in multiple immunology indications, while Dupixent earned >$14bn.

These types of drugs do not grow on trees, and it is simply far too early - in my view - to make positive comparisons between soquelitinib and "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) drugs like these.

Nevertheless, Soquelitinib is clearly the key value driver at Corvus today, and the drug does have demonstrable potential in both oncology and autoimmune, with intriguing catalysts on the horizon, as shown below:

The ALPS - Autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome - study is a Phase 2 study being conducted alongside the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases ("NIAID"), while a Phase 2 A study is scheduled to begin this year.

Financials

Corvus reported Q1 earnings last month. Loss from operations came to $(10m), but net income was reported as $15.2m as a result of the "early exercise of common stock warrants by stockholders providing cash proceeds of approximately $31.3 million". The cash position was $44m, which helps to explain Corvus's low market cap - $332, at the time of writing.

The company has limited funds, and has made net losses of $(62.3m), $(27m), and $(41.3m) in the past three years, working backwards. Based on this, investors should understand any investment in Corvus carries a high level of risk, not least because the company may soon need to raise further funding, likely via an at the market share issuance, which will dilute existing shareholders.

Last quarter, Corvus reported an R&D spend of $7.5m, and SG&A expense of $2.5, and an operating loss of $(9.9m). As such, the likelihood is Corvus would need to obtain more funding at the latest in early-to-mid 2026, at the current rate of cash burn, given the net income realized in Q1 is unlikely to be repeated.

Other Assets / Concluding Thoughts

Ciforadenant, an oral, small molecule antagonist of the A2A receptor for adenosine, is in an open label Phase 1b/2 clinical trial as a first line therapy for metastatic RCC in combination with ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) and nivolumab (anti-PD-1). The efficacy endpoints for the trial are deep response rate, defined as CR plus PRs of greater than 50% tumor volume reduction, as well as progression free survival.

Regarding Corvus, then, the positives are, firstly, the promise of the lead candidate, in two separate indications, a Phase 2 in AD planned to initiate this year, with interim data from the PTCL Phase 3 planned for "late 2026", according to the Q1 earnings call with analysts.

Secondly, management's experience developing a highly successful drug at Pharmacyclics (although my understanding is that neither CEO nor CFO were around when Pharmacyclics was sold to AbbVie), thirdly, a low valuation today, and Corvus' tendency to generate share price volatility, and finally, the risk mitigation provided by there being two other candidates of note within the pipeline, in ciforadenant and mupadolimab.

In summary, I would sum up Corvus as an undoubtedly risky opportunity, but an opportunity nonetheless. I don't think there is quite enough here to warrant a 'Buy' recommendation, and as mentioned, investors should be wary of <$500m market cap biotechs, especially where funding is limited, as it is in Corvus' case, and when assets have not yet been derisked by late stage studies.

I do find the PTCL study intriguing, as there is unmet need in this indication, and the responses achieved to date are quite impressive - six complete responses for example. Management's tendency to jump from opportunity to opportunity - shelving mupadolimab, for example, trialling soquelitinib in oncology and immunology, makes me wonder if management is flinging mud at the wall and hoping something sticks, but equally, it deserves praise for instigating the Phase 3 in PTCL, and who knows, perhaps the AD update due this year will generate another buying spree?

data.title: Crispr Therapeutics: Betting On A One-Shot Future
data.description:
It has been over a year since my previous Crispr Therapeutics AG (NASDAQ:CRSP) article, where I discussed the company's diversified gene-editing pipeline that was supported by a strong $2.1B cash position. At that time, CASGEVY had already received FDA approval, and the company was working with its partner, Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), on the commercial launch. I believe CASGEVY, in conjunction with the company’s pipeline covering oncology, autoimmune disorders, diabetes, and cardiovascular diseases, puts Crispr in a position to be a potential leader in gene-editing therapeutics. Although I was bullish on the company’s long-term growth, I acknowledged that CRSP was trading at a premium valuation, but the market has shaved some of that premium with the ticker being down roughly 35% since the publication of that article.

I have returned to Crispr Therapeutics after listening to the company’s recent Q&A session at the Goldman Sachs (GS) 46th Annual Global Healthcare Conference, which revealed the company is making progress in its transformation into commercial company with an impressive platform capable of multiple therapies in the coming years with huge opportunities lies in cardiovascular and autoimmune franchises. I am willing to take advantage of the selloff as the company continues to make steady progress.

I intend to review some of the company's recent updates and provide my views on CASGEVY, the company's cardiovascular franchise, and its recent partnership with Sirius Therapeutics. In addition, I will highlight some downside risks that investors need to consider when managing their CRSP position. Finally, I discuss my plans for my dormant CRSP position.

CASGEVY Keep Rolling

I'm encouraged by CASGEVY's methodical commercial rollout, which led to $14.2M in sales in Q1. The company and Vertex have activated over 65 authorized treatment centers around the world, with cell collections initiated for over 90 patients, and they have twice that in the referral pipeline.

Indeed, 90 patients are not exactly jaw-dropping numbers; however, I will use management's realistic framing of this launch when they compare it to medical device rollouts rather than a drug launch. Crispr and Vertex are selling a procedure, not a pill, which creates several hurdles to getting the infrastructure established and payer support. However, once things are in place, we should see an acceleration as treatment centers gain familiarity and increase their monthly throughput.

Therefore, we should see a steady ramp as long as the two companies can continue to execute and form a reliable revenue footing that can offset some expenses and allow the company to continue to invest in their platform.

Cardiovascular Franchise… The Big Hitter

The company's cardiovascular franchise is now my favorite aspect of Crispr, and the commercial prospects offer immense upside. The CTX-310 ANGPTL3 data was incredibly impressive, with a nearly 80% reduction in both LDL cholesterol and triglycerides from a single injection. Yes… that is correct. Comparing that to antibodies and siRNAs, which typically show ~60% triglyceride reduction with minimal LDL impact. So, at this point, it looks as if Crispr has the best-in-class efficacy. If that data holds up in bigger studies, that is a massive paradigm shift in cardiovascular and lipid management.

The CTX-320 LP(a) program could be transformative, due to Novartis's (NVS) HORIZON trial providing some external validation earlier this year. LP(a) is 6x more atherogenic than LDL cholesterol, so there should be both clinical and commercial demand for CTX-320. In fact, LP(a) levels are genetically determined at birth, so it should be relatively easy to identify eligible patients.

In the bargain, CTX-310 and CTX-320 are both wholly owned gene editing programs, so the company can offer a significant value proposition to payers due to the pharmaco-economics of a "one-and-done" gene therapy that could be superior to contemporary drugs that require repetitive dosing. Some of the new-age lipid-lowering therapies will most likely cost more throughout the patient’s lifetime, such as PCSK9 inhibitors (~$200K-$300K), Leqvio from Novartis (NVS) (calculated ~$325K), and Regeneron’s (REGN) Evkeeza ($450K-$460K per year). Not only do these come with high cumulative costs, but you are also concerned with the ongoing compliance challenges of the patient sticking to regular dosing.

What is more, some of the longstanding cheaper options, such as statins, have poor adherence and lackluster efficacy for high-risk populations. Meanwhile, both of Crispr’s therapies are one-time gene editing therapies potentially priced at $150K that are expected to be curative, meaning no recurring costs, eliminate adherence concerns, and could prevent expensive cardiovascular events, which makes it an attractive option for patients, providers, and even payers.

I don’t think I need to elaborate on the magnitude of the cardiovascular and lipid management markets, considering it is still the number one cause of death around the globe. However, I will note that despite all of the available drugs and treatments, there are still millions of patients who need options.

CTX310 represents a major commercial opportunity just in the U.S. alone. The company stated “In the U.S. alone, more than 40 million patients are affected by elevated LDL.” Admittedly, we don’t know the exact percentage of that population that will be eligible for the treatment, but I don’t think it’s crazy to expect peak annual sales between $3B and $6B if they stick with their estimated cost of $150K per dose. Indeed, the one-time treatment would shrink that population over time; however, if 100K patients globally were treated over a decade, the cumulative revenue could still hit $15B in that time period. Certainly, the label could be restricted to only rare conditions like homozygous FH, or Homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH), which affects roughly 26K people globally, but that would still give you $1.5B–$2B in cumulative revenue if just 10K patients get treated.

CTX320 has similar commercial prospects, considering roughly 20%-30% of people around the globe are expected to have elevated LP(a). Although we cannot expect that the entire population will be eligible for the treatment, you could still get global peak sales around $2B–$4B if it captures even 10%–20% of the LP(a)-elevated population (assuming CTX320 is priced around $150K).

Considering these points above, I think it is safe to say there is a possibility that both CTX-310 and CTX320 can achieve blockbuster status in short order, if approved.

Multi-Modal Strategy

I appreciate Crispr's strategy of building a multi-modal approach that allows them to employ several technologies to unlock a multitude of disease areas to target.

The company recently added another technology to its repertoire with the Sirius Therapeutics partnership, which gives Crispr access to what might be best-in-class siRNA technology.

SRSD107 is a long-acting siRNA that selectively inhibits Factor XI to decrease the likelihood of thrombotic events while also reducing the risk of bleeding. In a Phase I study, SRSD107 showed over 93% FXI reduction and sustained effects for up to six months.

It looks as if a Phase II trial is launching in total knee arthroplasty patients to confirm efficacy and hopefully generate a dosing for future studies. The deal between Crispr Therapeutics and Sirius Therapeutics is a co-development partnership for SRSD107 with a 50/50 profit. Crispr is paying $95M upfront but gains the U.S. commercialization rights.

It is hard to say if the market has recognized SRSD107’s commercial potential with several applications, including AFib, VTE, cancer-associated thrombosis, and dialysis patients. Even a modest 5%-10% adoption in these markets with pricing at $5K–$10K should yield a multi-billion dollar opportunity.

Risks to Consider

Despite my bullish outlook on Crispr, I must point out that the company still has several risks that investors should consider when managing their position. First, CASGEVY’s uptake is still in the early stages, and some of the commercial numbers are encouraging. However, there is going to be uncertainty concerning the therapy’s ability to scale quickly and its ceiling. Although CASGEVY is not a cornerstone of my long-term bull thesis, however, it does have some significance in the near term as it is the company’s only commercial product.

Although I don’t expect CASGEVY's revenue to completely cover the company’s expenses, I am expecting the revenue to grow enough to help at least offset some of the cash burn. Thankfully, the company finished Q1 with roughly $1.86B in total cash, and they averaged roughly $76.6M in cash burn per quarter over the past year. Assuming that stays the same, you are looking at roughly 6 years of cash runway. So, I am not stressing over the sales ramp for CASGEVY.

However, the commercial success of CASGEVY still has some weight, considering it might reset the Street's outlook for the company's gene therapies going forward. It is possible that the company produces stellar data for other therapies, but the Street has a "wait and see" stance on those programs until the commercial launch. Essentially, CASGEVY's success or failure could set the tone for the company's gene therapies.

The company’s other programs, including cardiovascular, allogeneic CAR-T, siRNA, autoimmune, oncology, and regenerative medicine, as well as partnered programs, are very promising, with massive upside. However, those programs are still in the early stages, and only some produce limited patient data. So, we don’t know if these programs have what it takes to get through the FDA with some of these novel indications and be a commercial success.

Considering these risks, I am maintaining my CRSP conviction level at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” portfolio.

My Strategy

In my previous Crispr article, I revealed that my CRSP position has been dormant as I decided to wait for additional data before resetting Buy and Sell Levels to manage my position. Since then, the company has posted some data, but the market's response has injected enough volatility into the ticker to "muddy up" the technical analysis that is needed to determine my levels. So, I am going to have to punt on formulating my new levels for the time being. However, that doesn't mean I'm sitting on the sidelines while I wait. For now, I am going to switch to a more catalyst-centric approach, where I will look to add on the sell-off or fade in the share price following an update.

Luckily, the company has a few catalysts on the docket for the remainder of the year. First, we have Phase I data from CTX‑310 that will be presented at a medical conference in the second half of the year. Obviously, any additional data or insight could be enough to move the share price and provide an opportunity to add to the position.

The company is also expecting a broad data update from both oncology and autoimmune for CTX112 in the middle of this year. In the bargain, we should also get an update on CTX131 for solid tumors and hematologic malignancies this year.

Of course, I will also remain diligent following earnings reports, where we will get CASGEVY revenue and uptake updates. Any choppiness in the numbers could trigger an unjustified sell-off that could be a chance to grab some CRSP shares.

For those looking to manage their positions in the immediate future, I would turn your attention to the Daily Chart, which shows bullish signals for CRSP. After bottoming out in April, the share price has set higher lows twice in May and is now testing the support/resistance level in the low $40s that we saw back in March of this year. The ticker has a “Very Bullish” rating on the Go-No-Go indicator and is trading above the anchored VWAP from the proximal high, as well as the 200-Day EMA. In addition, we can see a bullish divergence on the RSI, as well as some strengthening. So, I would say there are ingredients for a healthy reversal on the Daily Chart.

Admittedly, I won't be loading up on every catalyst, but I expect to be active in 2025 with the goal of attempting to generate some profit and get my CRSP position back to a “house money” status to hold for a long-term investment.

data.title: Solid Power: Slow Progress
data.description:
Solid Power, Inc. (NASDAQ:SLDP) has recently rallied back towards $2, but the investment story hasn't changed much with limited real catalysts this year. The company remains in a mostly pre-revenue state, working on promising solid-state battery ("SSB") technologies. My investment thesis remains Bullish on the stock trading at these levels, but the risk is high.

Prototype Phase

While SSBs offer a lot of promise, Solid Power exited Q1 planning to install the first globally known continuous manufacturing pilot line for sulfide electrolyte production at the SP2 facility. In essence, the company is still in the planning phase for a pilot line that won't be commissioned until 2026.

The biggest catalyst for the firm is working on the SK On operating a solid-state cell line designed by Solid Power to use their electrolyte. On the Q1 2025 earnings call, the CEO made this statement about the pilot line:

Our next corporate objective for this year is to continue to execute our agreements with SK On which support SK On's efforts to develop solid-state cells based on our technology and operate a solid-state pilot line that we design to use our electrolyte. We're close to completing our factory acceptance testing for the SK On line which is a key milestone under our line installation agreement with SK On. We expect to move to site acceptance testing of the line at SK On later this year.

As a reminder, Solid Power signed license and development agreements with SK On at the start of 2024 for the following amounts:

  • Research and development license for solid-state cell designs for $20 million from 2024 to 2027.
  • Line installation provides a new cell manufacturing line in exchange for $22 million.
  • Electrolyte supply of at least 8 metric tons through 2030 at a cost of $10 million.

Solid Power reported Q1 2025 revenues of $6 million, mostly from the deal with SK On. The company has shifted plans from building the cells to supplying the electrolyte material and licensing technology to cell manufacturers like SK On.

The SK On deal ultimately provides only $10 million in electrolyte sales. The pilot plant will expand production capacity for Solid Power to 75 metric tons per year of sulfide-based material for SSBs. The amounts will only provide material for small volume program testing.

At the InterBattery 2025 conference, Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) predicted a plan for full-scale mass production of all-SSBs not until 2030 to 2035 due to limiting construction methods. The outlined biggest fear was Chinese batter suppliers reaching mass production in 2027, ahead of the Korean battery firms.

The SSB market is clearly forecast to surge from less than $1 billion now to nearly $15 billion by 2034. The market forecast even appears relatively conservative, if SSBs enter the auto market to supply batteries for the EV revolution.

No Premium

Solid Power ended Q1 with total liquidity of nearly $300 million. The company burned just $29 million in the quarter, leaving the business with enough capital for a cash runway to reach towards commercialization plans in the 2026/27 time frame.

The SSB company avoids the expensive cell production manufacturing plants, costing in the billions. The issue is, leaving Solid Power reliant on cell manufacturers to choose their technology.

Back on the Q1 2025 earnings call, the CEO confirmed the company is looking at an extended period until material revenue, which might not occur until 2030:

We've talked about timings for significant electrolyte revenue in the past, as you know. We have a wide range of customers potentially as early as 2027, 2028, but the bulk of them are around 2030 and beyond.

The current consensus analyst estimates only have revenues in the $20 to $30 million range annually through 2027 or 2028. The stock only has a market cap of $320 million, which makes the investment case compelling.

Solid Power still needs additional capital, and the stock at only $2 could lead to massive share dilution. Also, battery technology is a risky area due to the unknown outcome of the winning technology, much less the electrolyte use even in the case of SSBs winning major market share.

The stock might trend lower in the short term, with the jump this week really due to limited news. One report had Solid Power involved with a pilot line in Chungiu in cooperation with Dahae Energy, and another report focused on the testing with BMW's (OTC:BMWKY) i7, something the company had disclosed back on May 20.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Solid Power is like a call option on the SSB sector when trading here below $2. The company has plenty of cash to support growth initiatives through the next couple of years, but investors should trade carefully here, with substantial downside risk on any setbacks in the sector or individually with the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cracks Are Spreading In The US Labor Market
data.description:
At first glance, the U.S. labor market appears to be strong. Unemployment is low by historical standards, jobs are being added, and the participation rate remains elevated.

But under the surface, something has changed.

Cracks are spreading, and the underlying data is telling a very different story—one that hasn’t yet made it into the headlines.

Understanding these subtle labor market shifts and what they mean about the business cycle can help you determine where the overall economy is heading and how it will impact asset prices, interest rate policy, and potentially even your business situation.

Over the past several years, the U.S. labor market, especially among the 25–to 54-year-old prime-age cohort, has been remarkably strong. Participation surged after the pandemic. Job quality improved, and both employment and hours worked hit new highs.

However, the data now signals a clear inflection point.

Let’s walk through the five key labor ratios that reveal exactly where we are in the cycle and where we might be headed next.

Prime-Age Labor Ratios

For these first three ratios, we will focus on the 25-54 year old population, known as the prime-age population, as this removes the effects of an aging populace and other demographic changes.

The first important indicator is the employment rate—that’s the share of the labor force that’s employed. This is simply the inverse of the unemployment rate.

The prime age employment rate has declined sharply from its cycle peak – this means there is more unemployment but it's likely not from widespread layoffs.

This metric usually starts to roll over early in the downturn, as firms begin to slow hiring. Although broad layoffs haven’t started yet, this early shift indicates that slack is quietly building.

Next is the employment-population ratio, which measures employment relative to the entire prime-age population, not just those in the labor force. This metric has also stalled and is now edging lower.

That tells us that job creation is no longer outpacing population growth. It’s a broader sign that the labor market is no longer absorbing workers at the rate it once was. Growth has plateaued—even if job losses or layoffs haven’t yet accelerated.

Then we have the labor force participation rate. This surged in recent years as workers re-entered the workforce. But it, too, has flattened, just shy of its cycle high.

This means that prime-age people are still engaged and workers haven’t dropped out of the labor force, but employers aren’t hiring at the same pace. So we’re getting a mismatch: strong or at least stable labor supply, but weakening demand.

Labor Quality Worsening

Now we’re going to dive even deeper into labor quality metrics, which is where the cracks in the labor market become even clearer.

When someone is working part-time for economic reasons, it means they actually want a full-time job but cannot find one. We can then examine the ratio of people working part-time for economic reasons as a percentage of all employed individuals.

This part-time for economic reasons ratio, which is inverted in the chart, has declined from its cycle peak. This means that an increasing share of the employed population is having trouble finding the full-time work they want and have to settle for part-time work.

A similar ratio, but slightly broader, is the full-time employment ratio. This refers to the proportion of the entire labor force that works full-time.

The full-time employment ratio is declining, which means a smaller share of the labor force is employed full-time. The ratio reached its highest level ever during the pandemic boom, as labor demand was intense; however, that has clearly shifted, and the signal is now clear: employers are reducing labor intensity.

This is a classic late-cycle labor market dynamic. Faced with slowing revenue growth, margin pressure, or a deteriorating demand outlook, firms often reduce costs by slowing hiring or limiting hours, especially in roles that are easiest to scale.

Instead of resorting to immediate layoffs, they shift workers to part-time positions, delay backfilling roles, or scale back full-time capacity, thereby preserving operational flexibility while managing payroll expenses.

One of the issues in this cycle is that these late-cycle labor dynamics have been present for almost two years. Normally, businesses cannot sustain this middle ground of throttling labor intensity without shifting to layoffs more quickly.

So why are businesses doing this now, and how have they been able to sustain it for so long?

Profit Margins

When profit margins are strong, companies can afford to retain staff and absorb softening demand.

Many firms saw profit margins surge post-COVID.

Let’s take an example from the residential construction sector. The average of the major US homebuilders saw profit margins explode from 10% before the pandemic to 19% at the peak. As the economy began to cool after 2022, those margins started to compress and fell back to around 15%, but still remain well above the pre-COVID level.

That extra margin cushion allows companies to slow hiring or reduce hours without laying off employees. It’s a way to cut costs incrementally, without disrupting operations or confidence.

That’s the phase we’re in now. Not yet an outright recessionary labor market, but a clear and sustained shift in labor demand, labor intensity, and the pace of employment growth.

Late Cycle Labor Dynamics

Altogether, these five labor market metrics paint a consistent picture and one of a classic late-cycle setup.

The U.S. labor market hasn’t collapsed—but it is clearly past the peak. This is the quiet phase where hiring slows and firms reduce hours without cutting headcount. These are the cracks that form before widespread layoffs.

If you understand these early labor market shifts, you can anticipate where the job market—and the broader economy—is heading.

data.title: Nvidia: All-Time High Is In Sight, Upgrade To Strong Buy
data.description:
Introduction

When I covered Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) at the tail-end of last year, I thought the stock was well set up going into 2025. Valuations had come down despite a furious run in the shares as earnings growth blistered ahead, buoyed by seemingly endless hyperscaler demand for GPUs. With 2025 set to be the year of Blackwell, I expected the stock to continue performing, albeit at more moderate pace than in the immediately preceding years.

Instead, we saw the stock briefly attempt to break to a new all-time high, or ATH, before a sharp turnaround and sell off. We know the various reasons for the reversal. On one hand the broader market sold off amidst tariff uncertainty leaving very few unscathed. The release of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) in the early part of 2025 led to a sort of flash panic, with claims that the boom in high end chips was over. The logic was DeepSeek would allow for such cost saving and efficiency that companies running AI inference could rely on lesser chips. I think the evidence at this point is that line of reasoning was just plain wrong, instead we saw the Jevons Paradox ring true.

Nvidia has also essentially now lost all China related revenue, something which perhaps was inevitable. Speaking at a recent conference, CFO Colette Kress pretty much threw cold water on the prospects of any further China revenue for the firm.

“Now that means where we stand is in a situation that we really don't have anything for that market. We've discussed that it wouldn't be appropriate for us to just start a new chip at this point because essentially, the H20 from where it compared to our Blackwell architecture was significantly lower in terms of what we were being able to enable in China. That was about a 25x change from an H20 to what you would receive in terms of a Blackwell.” (Colette Kress, CFO.)

With those headwinds consider, Nvidia has essentially gone nowhere since my last coverage as you can see from the below chart.

The price performance has indeed been a disappointment, but while the stock has been weighed by narratives such as DeepSeek, the earnings have just kept on grinding higher. The chart below shows the next twelve month, NTM EPS for Nvidia based on the consensus analyst forecast. I have annotated to show where EPS was at the time of my last coverage. As you can see its almost like a stairs, it has been consistently and periodically moving higher at near fixed intervals, despite the struggles in the stock's price. As a result of the lack of action in the price, the earnings growth has meant the valuation of the stock looks outright cheap.

The mixture of an extremely attractive valuation and a shifting narrative I believe sets Nvidia up nicely to have a better H2. The price momentum appears to be coming back into the stock as continued earnings growth, hyperscaler capex commentary and the new dynamic of sovereign AI has helped bring buying back into the name. I think Nvidia is well positioned heading into H2 and far beyond, after a period of consolidation, and I think the next leg is upon us. As a result, I am feeling even more confident and moving Nvidia to a Strong Buy rating.

Shareholder returns

As I see it Nvidia's valuation is overwhelmingly attractive. Its two best performing peers in the Magnificent-7 this year have been Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and Meta (NASDAQ:META). Looking at how Nvidia stacks up with these peers I see a great dispersion at present. Nvidia trades smack bang in the middle of the three in terms of its P/E ratio, but on the basis of both Return on Invested Capital and medium term EPS forecasts it is way ahead.

On the ROIC point, I think its important to remind readers why the metric is so important and what Nvidia's 80% return tells us about it. So I refer to the wonderful work of Michael Mauboussin from Morgan Stanley, who has written elegantly on this topic.

“A company creates value when the present value of the cash flows from its investments are greater than the cost of the investments. In other words, one dollar invested in the business becomes worth more than one dollar in the market.”

A company generates value for its shareholders when the return on the capital invested in the business exceeds the cost of capital, which is usually expressed as the weighted average cost of capital or WACC. That of course being the respective weights of debt and equity capital used in the business and their respective costs. On average for a high quality large cap name the WACC is likely somewhere between 8-12%.

So for simplicity sake let just assume for Nvidia the WACC is 10%, and the company as we know is generating a ROIC of 80%. This tells us that for every one dollar invested Nvidia is creating eight dollars of shareholder value. This kind of wealth creation is simply bananas. This is the hallmark of a "once in a generation" business, which is underpinned by one of the great investment cycles of our time, the AI revolution.

We know Nvidia has been powered ahead since 2022 by the huge capex cycle at the hyperscale cloud players. Each of which has thrown money hand over fist to secure GPUs to train proprietary AI models and serve inference on those models. This cycle shows no end in sight as the big tech players during the most recent earnings season reaffirmed their plans. For instance, Alphabet/Google (GOOG, GOOGL) is forking out $75bn this year, which would represent a huge 42% increase from 2024, which in turn saw a >60% capex increase from 2023. The pace of spending is ferocious.

On top of this demand, we now add in the new demand coming from sovereign AI. Last month we saw CEO Jensen Huang in the Middle East inking deals with Saudi Arabia and the UAE. Under these deals, Nvidia will provide 500,000 GPUs per year until 2027 to the UAE in partnership with domestic firms like G42. That's a lot of GPUs. The Saudi deal stipulated an initial deployment of 18,000 cutting edge Blackwell GB300s as part of a partnership with the new national AI company Humain. An announcement which one would imagine is simply the first step in the process given the scale of the UAE deal.

On the sovereign side I think this is the tip of the iceberg. The genie of U.S. tech proliferation is now out of the bottle following the loosening of restrictions on tech export to trusted partners. Nvidia does have to contend with the likelihood of never selling another chip to China but given this new wave of demand hitting the market, I think they are more than compensated.

Don't fall prey to bias

As the wonderful behavioral economist Richard Thaler set out, humans are prone to an array of cognitive bias. These are in essence, consistent mistakes we make in our judgment of the world, they are a function of our need to think quickly about the world, availing of so-called heuristics or short cuts to answers when evaluating the world.

This is relevant to Nvidia because of a thing called anchoring bias. This is a common cognitive error whereby we have a tendency to anchor predictions to currently available limits or levels, such as a price. Just look at any economists forecast and you'll see what I mean, generally the forecast is always some small variation on the current paradigm. It matters in the case of Nvidia because we are on the cusp of an all-time high and a valuation of greater than $3.5 trillion.

The human tendency, and bias, is to say, wow, 3.5 trillion, that's a big number. How much further can it go from here. The market cap of Nvidia is already close to the GDP of the UK. However, history teaches us these points of resistance are imaginary. Back in 2010, the largest stock in the S&P 500 (SP500) was Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM), which at the time boasted a market cap of around $315bn, and it was more than $50bn larger than second place Microsoft (MSFT), a huge margin at the time.

Investors at the time probably wondered realistically how much larger the biggest stocks could get. Exxon in this context has performed relatively poorly, rising just 53% since 2010. Microsoft, on the other hand, has seen a greater than 10x growth since then. If you had said to the average investor in 2010 that Microsoft would be over $3.5 trillion just 15 years later, you might have been laughed at or at least told to “be realistic.” The reason being, we are so grounded in the data in front of us today it can be hard to break the mold.

Despite a $3.5 trillion market cap today, I see absolutely no reason why Nvidia couldn't be worth more than $10 trillion either in 5 or 10 years time. The stock is generating unreal returns on shareholder capital, its P/E ratio is very low by the company's own history and somehow priced in line with big tech peers despite Nvidia forecast to deliver much higher EPS growth. As I see it the valuation looks really supportive of a strong buy recommendation here.

Risks

The biggest risk for Nvidia is the durability of capex spending. Decisions around capex traditionally at least have followed the economic cycle, companies opt to spend more when the going is good and pull back on spending in shakier times. The capex boom at hyperscalers has been a spending cycle like no other and thus far has shown little indication of letting up.

Of course, at some point the capex cycle will have to moderate. The question is to what degree. I think its unlikely we would see a sharp decline in capex spending, especially given the game theory dynamic of needing to spend if your rivals are spending, who in turn feel they need to spend if they think you will.

Competition remains a potential threat even if Nvidia appears to have a huge lead which is expected to persist. History tells the story many times over of market leaders assured of their success who grow complacent, see IBM (NASDAQ:IBM) in the 1980s or Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) more recently as examples.

Conclusion

I am pushing my recommendation on Nvidia to a Strong Buy. The stock has done very little from a price perspective since I covered it last. However, during this sideways action the underlying EPS growth has continued to grind higher, resulting in a very attractive P/E entry point. The sheer profitability of the company is a marvel and despite being one of the most valuable companies in the world today I think the runway remains in place for years to come.

The company has surfed the AI wave, underpinned by massive capex from hyperscalers pitted against each other for AI dominance. Now the market is expanding into the sovereign space with deals in Saudi Arabia and the UAE, deals which I believe will elongate one of the great capital investment cycles of our time. Nvidia remains the clear AI leader, competition is on the rise but remains a few steps behind. With the Rubin and Feynman chips already planned, I expect Nvidia will at least keep pace with competitors if not further extend their lead given a proven execution of product road maps.
